examples.title: Simulation of Multi-tape TM on Single-tape TM
examples.description: High-level algorithm for simulating a k-tape TM M on a single-tape TM. Uses separator symbols and position markers.
examples.explanation: The simulation maintains k virtual tapes on one physical tape using separators. Each simulated step requires two passes: first scanning to read all head positions, then scanning to update and move. This creates O(n) overhead per simulated step, giving O(n²) total for O(n) original runtime.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n²) worst-case when simulating O(n) MTM
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) - same as input plus separators and markers

examples.title: NDTM Simulation using BFS on Configuration Tree
examples.description: Simulating a Non-deterministic TM using deterministic BFS exploration of its configuration tree.
examples.code:
// Input: NDTM N = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q₀, q_accept, q_reject), input w
// Uses tape to store configuration tree, BFS explores level by level

BEGIN
  // Initialize root configuration
  WRITE (q₀, w, position 0)  // Initial config on tape
  
  WHILE configurations_exist DO
    // Get next configuration at current BFS level
    current_config ← GET_NEXT_CONFIG()
    
    // Extract state, tape content, head position from config
    state ← current_config.state
    tape ← current_config.tape
    pos ← current_config.head_position
    
    IF state = q_accept THEN
      ACCEPT  // Found accepting path
      HALT
    END IF
    
    IF state = q_reject THEN
      // This branch rejects, continue exploring other branches
      CONTINUE
    END IF
    
    // Get all possible transitions from δ(state, symbol_at_pos)
    possible_moves ← δ(state, tape[pos])
    
    FOR each move in possible_moves DO
      // Apply transition: new_state, new_symbol, direction
      new_tape ← COPY(tape)
      new_tape[pos] ← new_symbol
      new_pos ← pos + (direction = 'R' ? 1 : -1)
      new_config ← (new_state, new_tape, new_pos)
      
      // Add to queue for next BFS level
      ADD_TO_QUEUE(new_config)
    END FOR
  END WHILE
  
  // No accepting configuration found in entire tree
  REJECT
END
examples.explanation: The DTM explores the configuration tree using BFS to find any accepting path. Each configuration encodes complete state of the NDTM. The simulation explores all branches at depth d before going to depth d+1, guaranteeing that if any path accepts, it will be found.
examples.timeComplexity: O(b^T(n)) where b = maximum branching factor and T(n) = NDTM runtime

examples.title: Universal Turing Machine - Core Simulation Loop
examples.description: The main simulation loop of a UTM that interprets and executes any encoded Turing Machine M on input w.
examples.code:
// UTM input: <M>#w where <M> is encoding of TM M, w is input
// UTM state set includes: find_state, find_symbol, apply_transition, accept, reject

BEGIN
  // Main simulation loop
  WHILE TRUE DO
    // Step 1: Locate current state marker on tape
    FIND_STATE_MARKER(current_state)
    
    // Step 2: Find symbol under the (simulated) head
    current_symbol ← READ_UNDER_HEAD()
    
    // Step 3: Look up transition in encoded δ table
    // <M> contains δ as: (state, symbol) → (new_state, new_symbol, direction)
    transition ← LOOKUP_TRANSITION(current_state, current_symbol)
    
    // Step 4: Apply the transition
    WRITE_SYMBOL(transition.new_symbol)
    MOVE_HEAD(transition.direction)
    REMOVE_STATE_MARKER(current_state)
    PLACE_STATE_MARKER(transition.new_state)
    
    // Step 5: Check for halting states
    IF transition.new_state = q_accept THEN
      ACCEPT
      HALT
    END IF
    
    IF transition.new_state = q_reject THEN
      REJECT
      HALT
    END IF
    
    // Continue simulation loop
  END WHILE
END

// Supporting procedures
FIND_STATE_MARKER(s):
  SCAN_TAPE_LEFT_TO_RIGHT
  LOCATE marker_for_state_s
  RETURN position

LOOKUP_TRANSITION(s, a):
  SEARCH_IN_ENCODED_TABLE(s, a)
  RETURN (new_state, new_symbol, direction)
examples.explanation: The UTM is a universal interpreter. It maintains encoded rules of M and applies them to the encoded input. Each iteration represents one step of the simulated machine. The UTM halts in accept/reject when the simulated machine enters its halting states. This demonstrates that one machine can compute anything any specifically designed machine can compute.
examples.timeComplexity: If M runs in T(n) steps, UTM runs in O(T(n) · |M|) - linear overhead in the size of M's encoding
examples.spaceComplexity: O(T(n) + |M|) - requires tape for M's description plus simulation space
